subject: A Review of Set Theory
question: Show that if $A, B$, and $C$ are subsets of a universe $U$ then $n(A\cup B\cup C) = n(A) + n(B) + n(C) - n(A\cap B) - n(A\cap C) - n(B\cap C) + n(A\cap B\cap C)$.
answer: Using Theorem 2.3, we find $n(A\cup B\cup C) = n(A\cup (B\cup C)) = n(A) + n(B\cup C) - n(A\cap (B\cup C)) = n(A) + (n(B) + n(C) - n(B\cap C)) - n((A\cap B)\cup (A\cap C)) = n(A) + (n(B) + n(C) - n(B\cap C)) - (n(A\cap B) + n(A\cap C) - n(A\cap B\cap C)) = n(A) + n(B) + n(C) - n(A\cap B) - n(A\cap C) - n(B\cap C) + n(A\cap B\cap C)$
explanation: Answer: Using Theorem 2.3, we find $n(A\cup B\cup C) = n(A\cup (B\cup C)) = n(A) + n(B\cup C) - n(A\cap (B\cup C)) = n(A) + (n(B) + n(C) - n(B\cap C)) - n((A\cap B)\cup (A\cap C)) = n(A) + (n(B) + n(C) - n(B\cap C)) - (n(A\cap B) + n(A\cap C) - n(A\cap B\cap C)) = n(A) + n(B) + n(C) - n(A\cap B) - n(A\cap C) - n(B\cap C) + n(A\cap B\cap C)$
